items.question_text: Under 8-1-6, when a position report affecting separation is not received, take action to obtain the report no later than _____ minutes after the control estimate, unless otherwise specified.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, the deadline is 10 minutes after the control estimate.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-6, how does the oceanic missing position report deadline differ from the domestic deadline in Chapter 5?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, oceanic operations use 10 minutes after the control estimate. Domestic radar rules in Chapter 5 use a 5-minute deadline for overdue aircraft.

choices.text: Oceanic operations allow 5 minutes after the control estimate, the same as domestic radar rules.
Oceanic operations use 10 minutes after the control estimate — double the domestic 5-minute deadline.
Oceanic operations require immediate action regardless of estimate, unlike domestic rules.
Oceanic operations use 3 minutes, stricter than domestic rules.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-6, the obligation to obtain a missing position report applies only when the report affects separation. A trainee argues that ALL missed position reports require action within 10 minutes regardless of separation impact. Are they correct?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, the rule triggers only when the report affects separation. Reports that do not affect separation are not covered by this paragraph's timing requirement.

choices.text: No — the rule only applies when a position report affecting separation is not received.
Yes — all missed position reports require action, regardless of separation impact.
Yes — but the deadline is 5 minutes for non-separation reports.
No — oceanic reports never trigger action obligations.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-7, FAA Order JO _____ establishes procedures for reporting oceanic errors.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-7, FAA Order JO 7210.632 establishes the reporting procedures.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-7, which of the following is NOT listed as an oceanic error category reported under JO 7210.632?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-7, the listed categories are Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and SAO verification. Communication failures are not separately listed.

choices.text: Gross Navigation Errors (GNE).
Height errors.
Communication failure events.
Special Area of Operations (SAO) verification.

items.question_text: A controller observes a time-based longitudinal error in oceanic airspace and wants to know whether to report it. Under 8-1-7, is a time-based longitudinal error a reportable category?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-7, time (longitudinal) errors are among the explicitly listed reportable categories under JO 7210.632.

choices.text: No — only gross navigation errors are reportable.
Yes — time (longitudinal) errors are explicitly listed as a reportable category.
Only if the error resulted in a loss of separation.
Only if reported by the pilot, not the controller.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-8, control estimates may be determined by the ATC automation system or calculated by the controller using known wind patterns, previous aircraft transit times, pilot progress reports, and _____.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, controller calculation also uses pilot estimates as one input.

choices.text: pilot estimates

items.question_text: Under 8-1-8, which of the following is NOT a listed source for determining control estimates?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, sources are the ATC automation system in use, or calculation by the controller using known wind patterns, previous aircraft transit times, pilot progress reports, and pilot estimates. NOTAM data is not listed.

choices.text: ATC automation system.
NOTAM data for en route weather.
Known wind patterns.
Previous aircraft transit times.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-8, can control estimates be updated after they are initially determined?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, control estimates may be updated through automated position reports and/or manually by the controller.

choices.text: No — the initial estimate is frozen and may never be changed.
Yes — through automated position reports and/or manual updates by the controller.
Only through pilot-requested amendments to the flight plan.
Only by supervisor override after the first hour of flight.

items.question_text: A trainee says that pilot estimates and pilot progress reports serve the same function as control estimates under 8-1-8. How should you correct this misconception?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, pilot estimates and progress reports are inputs the controller may use to calculate control estimates. They are not the same thing as control estimates — control estimates are the controller's or automation's time-referenced position determination.

choices.text: Pilot estimates and progress reports are data sources used to calculate control estimates; they are not the same thing as the control estimate itself.
They are identical — the paragraph treats pilot reports and control estimates as interchangeable.
Pilot estimates override control estimates and must be used instead.
Control estimates may never use pilot-provided data.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-9, non-RVSM aircraft may not be permitted in RVSM airspace unless they meet the criteria of excepted aircraft and are previously approved by the _____.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-9a, approval must come from the operations supervisor/CIC.

choices.text: operations supervisor/CIC

items.question_text: Under 8-1-9, which oceanic non-RVSM exception is geographically limited to the Oakland, Anchorage, and Arctic FIRs?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-9b4, the spare-engine-under-wing exception applies only within the Oakland, Anchorage, and Arctic FIRs.

choices.text: An aircraft being initially delivered to the State of Registry.
An aircraft being utilized for mercy or humanitarian purposes.
An aircraft transporting a spare engine mounted under the wing.
An aircraft formerly RVSM-approved being flown to maintenance.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-9, the exceptions for non-RVSM aircraft operating in RVSM airspace are accommodated on a _____ basis.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-9b4(a), exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis.

choices.text: workload or traffic-permitting

items.question_text: Under 8-1-9, in addition to the oceanic non-RVSM exceptions listed in 8-1-9b, where else are excepted aircraft listed for RVSM operations?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-9b and its reference block, the oceanic exceptions are in addition to those listed in paragraph 2-1-29, RVSM Operations.

choices.text: Paragraph 2-1-29, RVSM Operations.
Paragraph 8-1-10, Weather Deviations.
14 CFR 91.155, Basic VFR weather minimums.
Paragraph 5-1-1, Application of Radar.

items.question_text: A controller has a heavy workload and a non-RVSM aircraft requests entry into RVSM airspace for a mercy flight. The aircraft qualifies as excepted and has prior supervisor approval. Under 8-1-9, must the controller accommodate the exception?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-9b4(a), non-RVSM exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis. Even an approved excepted aircraft may be denied entry if workload or traffic does not permit.

choices.text: No — exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis, so the controller may decline if workload is too heavy.
Yes — once the exception is approved, the controller must accept the aircraft regardless of workload.
Yes — mercy flights always take absolute priority over all other traffic.
No — mercy flights are never excepted in oceanic RVSM airspace.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-10, weather deviation requests should take priority over _____ requests.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-10, weather deviation requests should take priority over routine requests since aircraft will not fly into adverse meteorological conditions.

choices.text: routine

items.question_text: Under 8-1-10, when there is conflicting traffic and ATC is unable to establish required separation for a weather deviation, what are the three actions ATC must take in order?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-10, the three steps are: a) advise the pilot unable to issue clearance for requested deviation; b) advise the pilot of conflicting traffic; and c) request pilot's intentions.

choices.text: Advise unable, advise of conflicting traffic, and request pilot's intentions.
Request intentions first, then advise traffic, then issue unable if no resolution found.
Issue a holding clearance, then advise traffic, then vector the aircraft.
Advise traffic first, then approve deviation if the pilot accepts responsibility, then request intentions.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-10, if communications cannot be established or a revised ATC clearance is not available after a weather deviation, what procedures should pilots follow?
items.explanation: Per the note in 8-1-10, pilots follow procedures in AIP Section ENR 7.3 and AC 91-70.

choices.text: Procedures in AIP Section ENR 7.3 and AC 91-70, Oceanic and Remote Continental Airspace Operations.
Standard lost-communication procedures in 14 CFR 91.185 only.
The pilot must land at the nearest suitable airport immediately.
Any deviation the pilot deems necessary without further coordination.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-10, the phraseology for denying a weather deviation requires telling the pilot the conflicting aircraft's call sign, position, altitude, and _____. Fill in the missing element.
items.explanation: Per the phraseology block, the transmission includes call sign, position, altitude, and direction.

choices.text: direction

items.question_text: A controller receives two simultaneous requests: a weather deviation request from one aircraft and a routine route amendment from another. One trainee says to handle the routine request first because it came in first. Under 8-1-10, what is the correct priority?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-10, weather deviation requests should take priority over routine requests because aircraft will not fly into adverse weather.

choices.text: Handle the routine request first because it was received first.
Give the weather deviation request priority over the routine request.
Stack both requests and handle them in altitude order.
Deny both until one of the aircraft declares an emergency.

items.question_text: Two position reports are missing. One affects separation between aircraft; the other does not. Which requires action under 8-1-6?

choices.text: Both require action within 10 minutes of the control estimate.
Only the position report affecting separation triggers the 10-minute action requirement.
Neither requires action unless the aircraft are on crossing tracks.
Only the report that does not affect separation, because separation-related reports have a different rule.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, the action obligation applies when a position report affecting separation is not received.

items.situation: An aircraft in oceanic airspace has not sent a position report that affects separation between it and a following aircraft on the same track. The control estimate was 8 minutes ago, and no other timing specification applies.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-6, when a position report affecting separation is not received, take action to obtain the report no later than 10 minutes after the control estimate unless otherwise specified.

choices.text: Take action now to obtain the report before the 10-minute deadline passes.
Wait until 10 minutes have passed exactly before taking any action.
Wait for the next routine contact because 8 minutes is not yet a concern.
Assume the aircraft crossed the fix and update separation based on the estimate alone.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, the controller may take action at any point up to the 10-minute deadline. Since 8 minutes have passed, action should be taken before the deadline expires.

items.question_text: What is the operational purpose for reporting oceanic navigation, height, and time errors under JO 7210.632?

choices.text: The data is used for risk modeling activities that support separation standard reductions.
The data is used only for individual controller performance evaluations.
The data is used to determine whether to close an oceanic sector.
The data is used only for billing airlines for navigation services.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-7, the oceanic error-report data is needed for risk modeling activities to support separation standard reductions.

items.situation: You observe an aircraft in oceanic airspace that is 500 feet off its assigned flight level in RVSM airspace. A trainee asks if this qualifies as a reportable height error under the oceanic error reporting program.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-7, FAA Order JO 7210.632 establishes procedures for reporting Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations verification in oceanic airspace.

choices.text: Follow the JO 7210.632 reporting procedures because height errors are a listed reportable category.
No report is needed because height errors are only reportable if separation is lost.
Report it as a gross navigation error since the aircraft is off altitude.
Ignore it because height errors are only reportable in domestic airspace.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-7, height errors are among the reportable categories under JO 7210.632.

items.question_text: A controller is applying 10-minute longitudinal separation in oceanic airspace. What must the controller use to determine aircraft positions?

choices.text: Control estimates, because they must be used when applying time-based separation minima.
Only the last received pilot position report, regardless of how old it is.
The flight plan filed ETA without any updates or controller calculation.
Any method the controller prefers, since time-based separation does not require estimates.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, control estimates must be used when applying time-based separation minima.

items.situation: You are establishing time-based separation between two oceanic aircraft. The automation system provides one estimate, but a pilot position report suggests a different position. The automation estimate appears more recent.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-8, control estimates may be determined by the ATC automation system or calculated by the controller using known wind patterns, previous transit times, pilot progress reports, and pilot estimates. Estimates may be updated through automated position reports or manually.

choices.text: Use the automation system estimate but verify it against available data; control estimates may be updated through automated position reports or manually as needed.
Discard the automation estimate and use only the pilot report because pilot data always supersedes automation.
Average the two positions and use the midpoint for all separation calculations.
Ignore both and use only the filed flight plan time for separation.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, control estimates may be determined by automation or calculated by the controller using various inputs including pilot reports. The controller may update estimates as better information becomes available.

items.question_text: Which four oceanic operations are listed as excepted non-RVSM aircraft under 8-1-9?

choices.text: Initial deliveries; maintenance ferry flights for failed RVSM equipment; mercy or humanitarian flights; and spare-engine-under-wing transport within Oakland, Anchorage, and Arctic FIRs.
Medical evacuation; military intercept; search and rescue; and aerial refueling.
State aircraft; aircraft below FL290; aircraft on delivery flights; and aircraft with an inoperative transponder.
Cargo-only flights; repositioning flights; training flights; and aircraft without TCAS.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-9b, the four oceanic non-RVSM exceptions are: initial deliveries, maintenance ferry flights for failed RVSM equipment, mercy/humanitarian flights, and spare-engine-under-wing transport in Oakland/Anchorage/Arctic FIRs.

items.situation: A non-RVSM aircraft is being delivered from the manufacturer to its first operator and requests transit through oceanic RVSM airspace. The operations supervisor has approved the exception. Traffic is light.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-9, non-RVSM aircraft may not be permitted in RVSM airspace unless they meet excepted aircraft criteria and are previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC. Initial delivery aircraft are listed as excepted. Exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis.

choices.text: The aircraft may be permitted because it qualifies as an excepted aircraft (initial delivery), has supervisor approval, and workload/traffic conditions permit.
Deny the request because initial delivery aircraft are never excepted in oceanic airspace.
Permit the aircraft only if it files IFR and descends below FL290.
Permit the aircraft without any approval because delivery flights are automatically exempt.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-9, initial delivery aircraft are listed as excepted, require prior supervisor/CIC approval, and are accommodated on a workload/traffic-permitting basis.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-10, when no conflicting traffic exists horizontally and an aircraft requests a weather deviation, what must ATC do?

choices.text: Issue clearance to deviate from track.
Establish vertical separation first, then issue the deviation clearance.
Advise the pilot to request deviation from the next sector.
Deny the request because weather deviations are not permitted in oceanic airspace.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-10, if there is no traffic in the horizontal dimension, ATC must issue clearance to deviate from track.

items.question_text: A pilot asks what procedures to follow if communications are lost after a weather deviation in oceanic airspace. Under 8-1-10, which publications contain these procedures?

choices.text: AIP Section ENR 7.3, Special Procedures for In-flight Contingencies in Oceanic Airspace, and AC 91-70, Oceanic and Remote Continental Airspace Operations.
14 CFR 91.185, IFR operations: Two-way radio communications failure.
FAA Order JO 7210.632, Air Traffic Organization Occurrence Reporting.
AIM Chapter 6, Emergency Procedures Available to All Pilots.

items.explanation: Per the note in 8-1-10, pilots follow AIP ENR 7.3 and AC 91-70 for oceanic in-flight contingencies when communications cannot be established.
